items.text: Right Honorable, I know not how I shall offend in dedicating my unpolished lines to your Lordship, nor how the world will censure me for choosing so strong a prop to support so weak a burden; only if your Honor seem but pleased, I account myself highly praised and vow to take advantage of all idle hours till I have honored you with some graver labor.
items.context: Dedication to Henry Wriothesley, Earl of Southampton, in the first published version of Venus and Adonis, expressing hesitation about publishing his work and seeking patronage
items.publicReaction: The poem was popular and went through multiple editions; dedications to patrons were conventional practice and sought to secure ongoing financial support
items.source: Venus and Adonis, Folger Shakespeare Library, original 1594 publication

items.text: The love I dedicate to your Lordship is without end; whereof this pamphlet without beginning is but a superfluous moiety. The warrant I have of your honorable disposition, not the worth of my untutored lines, makes it assured of acceptance. What I have done is yours; what I have to do is yours; being part in all I have, devoted yours.
items.context: Dedication to Henry Wriothesley, Earl of Southampton, in The Rape of Lucrece, expressing Shakespeare's devotion to his patron
items.publicReaction: The dedication demonstrated Shakespeare's secure patronage relationship with Southampton; such dedications were important for establishing an author's social standing
items.source: The Rape of Lucrece, Folger Shakespeare Library, original 1594 publication

items.text: In the name of God amen I William Shakespeare of Stratford upon Avon in the county of Warwick gentleman in perfect health and memory God be praised do make and ordain this my last will and testament in manner and form following. That is to say first I commend my soul into the hands of God my creator, hoping and assuredly believing through the only merits of Jesus Christ my Saviour to be made partaker of life everlasting, and my body to the earth whereof it is made.
items.context: Opening statement of Shakespeare's last will and testament, expressing religious faith and declaring his sound mind at the time of writing
items.publicReaction: The will became a significant historical document; the bequest of the 'second-best bed' to his wife sparked later interpretation and debate about marital relationships
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented (Folger Shakespeare Library)

items.text: Item I gyve unto my wief my second best bed w[i]th the furniture
items.context: Specific bequest in Shakespeare's will to his wife Anne, with furniture (bedcover and hangings)
items.publicReaction: This bequest generated significant scholarly debate over centuries about Shakespeare's feelings toward his wife; modern scholars note it was actually a valuable and customary bequest
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: Item I give to my daughter Judith one hundred and fifty pounds of lawful English money to be paid unto her in manner and form following, that is to say, one hundred pounds in discharge of her marriage portion within one year after my decease... and as much more to be paid her when she shall be married.
items.context: Bequest to his younger daughter Judith in his will, with conditions tied to her marriage
items.publicReaction: The specific financial arrangement suggests Shakespeare sought to secure his daughter's future and provide for her marriage
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: Item I give and bequeath to my daughter Susanna Hall all my lands, tenements and hereditaments situate, lying and being in the town of Stratford upon Avon in the county of Warwick... to have and to hold all and singular the said lands, tenements and hereditaments with their appurtenances to the said Susanna Hall for and during the term of her natural life.
items.context: Bequest of his primary estate and properties in Stratford to his eldest daughter Susanna and her husband John Hall
items.publicReaction: This arrangement secured the majority of his estate for his eldest daughter, which was standard practice for entailment of property
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: To Mr William Shakespeare a bequest of 26 shillings and 8 pence to buy a mourning ring
items.context: Shakespeare's bequest to his theatrical colleagues Richard Burbage, John Heminges, and Henry Condell in his will, to purchase mourning rings
items.publicReaction: The specific bequest to his fellow actors demonstrated Shakespeare's regard for his colleagues and professional solidarity with the King's Men
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: He knowethe the parties plaintiff and defendant for the space of about ten years
items.context: Shakespeare's sworn deposition in the Bellott v Mountjoy case at the Court of Requests, answering the first interrogatory about his acquaintance with the parties
items.publicReaction: The discovery of this deposition in 1909 provided one of the few recorded instances of Shakespeare's spoken words under oath
items.source: Bellott v Mountjoy: First set of depositions, on Bellott's behalf, including Shakespeare's signature. Shakespeare Documented (National Archives, Kew)

items.text: the complainant did well and honestly behave himselfe and was a very good and industrious servant
items.context: Shakespeare's sworn testimony in the Bellott v Mountjoy case, characterizing Stephen Bellott's service to Christopher Mountjoy
items.publicReaction: This testimony was favorable to Bellott but not sufficiently detailed to resolve the financial dispute
items.source: Bellott v Mountjoy: First set of depositions, Shakespeare Documented

items.text: the defendant's wife did sollicitt and entreat this deponent to move and persuade the said Complainant to effect the said Marriage and accordingly this deponent did move and persuade the complainant therunto
items.context: Shakespeare's sworn testimony in the Bellott v Mountjoy case, confirming his role as intermediary in arranging the marriage between Stephen Bellott and Mary Mountjoy
items.publicReaction: This testimony established Shakespeare as a witness to important family negotiations and financial arrangements
items.source: Bellott v Mountjoy: First set of depositions, Shakespeare Documented

items.text: the defendant promised the plaintiff a portion of monie and goods in Marriage with Mary his daughter, but this deponent doth not remember the certainty of the sum
items.context: Shakespeare's sworn testimony in the Bellott v Mountjoy case, stating that he could not recall the specific amount of the marriage portion promised
items.publicReaction: This admission of imperfect memory was crucial to the case outcome, as Shakespeare could not corroborate the financial claims
items.source: Bellott v Mountjoy: First set of depositions, Shakespeare Documented

items.text: I give to the poor of the parish of Holy Trinity the sum of ten pounds
items.context: Charitable bequest in Shakespeare's will to the poor of his parish church in Stratford-upon-Avon
items.publicReaction: This charitable gift demonstrated Shakespeare's integration into his home parish and concern for community welfare
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented
